text: Welcome to this video presentation.
We would like to demonstrate how additional length for allele pouch anal endostomosis can be obtained to make the pouch reach without tension.
At the same time, we aim to demonstrate the potential value of fluorescence angiography in this setting to assert an adequate perfusion.
The case we would like to present regards a twenty-three year old female patient diagnosed with ulcerative colitis in two thousand and thirteen.
Due to biological refractory disease, she underwent a subtotal colectomy and ileostomy procedure in May, two thousand and twenty.
After convalescence, she was planned for a modified two-stage pouch procedure.
One of the Achilles heels of pouch surgery is anastomotic leakage.
Insufficient reach of the pouch is a risk factor for developing anastomotic leakage due to traction on the anastomosis.
For this reason, routine intraoperative lengthening measures are performed.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesenteric root of the small bowel up to the duodenum, making transverse peritoneal incisions approximately every three centimeters.
This flowchart represents the pathway of lengthening measures.
The yardstick of sufficient reach of the pouch is two centimeters below the lower border of the pubic bone.
If these routine lengthening measures do not suffice to obtain adequate reach, additional lengthening measures are required.
The surgeon considers options for increasing bowel reach, including ligation of the ileocolic vascular trunk or the interconnecting terminal ileobranches.
Taking vascular branches to obtain additional length has the inherent risk to compromise the perfusion of the pouch by either compromising inflow or outflow.
The terminal ileum is visibly exteriorized through the old ileostomy site, showing its position outside the abdominal cavity.
The video shows the ileocolic artery, which was previously ligated during the subtotal colectomy, as part of the surgical procedure.
White arrows indicate traction caused by the interconnecting terminal ileal branches, highlighting their role in the surgical field.
Translumination reveals the vascular arcade within the terminal ileal mesentery, highlighting the interconnected blood vessels in the area.
The surgeon assesses the macroscopic perfusion of the terminal ileum by clamping the interconnecting terminal ileal branches, observing the tissue response prior to ligation.
The surgeon ligates the interconnecting terminal ileal branches to obtain additional mesenteric length, facilitating the reach of the pouch.
The surgeon ligates the arterial supply of the terminal ileum, which may visually affect the perfusion of the pouch.
The surgeon performs a white light assessment of the terminal ileum, revealing no visible perfusion anomalies in the tissue.

Following the creation of the pouch, the surgeon injects a 0.1 mg/kg bolus of NDC-9 green intravenously. Fluorescence angiography imaging is performed using a Stryker 1688 AIM camera, revealing the vascular structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes fluorescent enhancement in the terminal ileum, which occurs 28 seconds after the intravenous injection of NDC nine green, indicating adequate perfusion.
The fluorescent overlay image reveals suboptimal perfusion at the tip of the pouch, indicated by reduced fluorescence intensity in that specific area.
The surgeon reinforces the area at the tip of the pouch with separate PDS-IV-Zero sutures, ensuring secure closure.
The preoperative plan for a modified two-stage procedure was also changed into a three-stage procedure, as a protective ileostomy was created, even though there was now sufficient length.
The endoluminal assessment reveals a well-vascularized and intact blind loop on the left side of the image, while the right side shows the anastomotic site two weeks postoperatively.
Thank you for your attention.
